text: The distribution of ions at the air/water interface is highly relevant to numerous chemical and environmental phenomena. Long-standing wisdom in the scientific community is that large polarizable ions are surface active and that these ions accumulate at the air/water surface headspace to generate interfacial electric fields that influence structure and arrangement of interfacial water molecules. Here, we have challenged this prevailing wisdom using heterodyne-detected vibrational sum-frequency generation (VSFG) and neural network-assisted ab initio molecular dynamics simulations. The results show that the distribution of ions in common electrolyte solutions is not as previously assumed, but that ions are situated at some subsurface depth. This generates a stratified interfacial structure with a very clean and pure ion depleted air/water interface headspace and an ion rich subsurface layer, thereby introducing new insights into ion-induced structuring of the air/water interface.
